Watkinson /Herkenne /Guy /Hadgraft / Oliveira /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:15–21 16 where J represents drug flux, D is the drug diffusion co- efficient, K is the partition coefficient for drug between the stratum corneum and the vehicle, C vehicle is the drug concentration in the vehicle and h is the diffusional path length for the drug. If a cosolvent does not affect membrane barrier prop- erties, its inclusion in the formulation of a weakly acidic drug of the form HX is expected to (1) increase the unionized fraction (HX) of the drug. Since the latter has a higher lipophilicity relative to X – , the overall skin flux, which is proportional to the skin-formulation partition coefficient, K (equation 1) should theoretically be enhanced ; (2) increase drug solubility in the formulation. If the drug concentration is kept constant, this effectively de- creases the degree of saturation and hence should lead to a lower flux. The precise outcome will depend ulti- mately on the nature of the cosolvent, the pH of the formulation and the pK a of the drug. Many non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are or- ganic acids with pK a s ranging from 3 to 5. At the pH of the skin surface (  5), such compounds will be ionized. In the deeper layers of the skin, a less acidic environment is encountered and the degree of ionization of such mol- ecules will therefore increase with consequent effects on solubility and permeation. Ibuprofen is one of the most widely available non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for the management of inflammation and is available in both gel and cream formulations. Although the perme- ation of ibuprofen has been studied from a range of ve- hicles [2] , there has been, to our knowledge, no system- atic investigation into the manner in which formulation components such as cosolvents may influence the ioniza- tion and permeability of ibuprofen into and through the skin. The aim of the present study therefore was to exam- ine the influence of increasing amounts of the cosolvent ethanol on the solubility, ionization and permeation characteristics of ibuprofen through silicone and human skin. As saturated solutions were used throughout this work, a further aim was to investigate the possibility of self-association of ibuprofen in the different cosolvent mixtures. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health- care, Havant, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility of Ibuprofen in Aqueous-Ethanol Mixtures Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were then centrifuged for 15 min at 4,000 g and the supernatant was diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was employed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml  min –1 . The retention time was approximate- ly 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis, and CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Saturated Aqueous Solubility as a Function of pH Excess drug was added to 10 ml of phosphate-citrate buffer [3] at different pH values and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath maintained at 32 ° C. The ionic strength was con- trolled by inclusion of 3 M NaClO 4 in the buffer solutions. Solu- tions were centrifuged for 30 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was diluted and assayed by UV spectroscopy. Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with SD and CV were calculated. pK a and log P and log D Determination The pK a values of ibuprofen were determined using published methods [4, 5] . Ibuprofen was titrated in mixtures of water and ethanol using a GlpKa apparatus (Sirius Analytical Instruments, Forrest Row, UK). The ionic strengths of the solutions were set at 0.15 M with KCl. Yasuda-Shedlovsky plots were used to calculate ‘aqueous’ pK a values [6, 7] . Using octanol as the partition solvent, log P values were also determined by potentiometric titration. The pK a log P program (version 5.1, ACD Ltd.) was also used to predict the distribution coefficients (log D) which were subse- quently compared with the experimentally derived values. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m as de- scribed previously [8] . Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery, obtained following informed consent and with appropri- ate institutional ethical approval was used for skin permeation studies. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [9] . The tissue was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was very carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was immersed in cold water, mounted onto filter paper, and stored in a freezer at –20 ° C until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments (n 6 4) were conducted in Franz dif- fusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, and a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline. The diffusion experiments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. Studies were also con- Downloaded by: Stockholm University Library 130.237.165.40 - 11/14/2018 6:09:37 PM

Watkinson /Herkenne /Guy /Hadgraft / Oliveira /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:15–21 16 where J represents drug flux, D is the drug diffusion co- efficient, K is the partition coefficient for drug between the stratum corneum and the vehicle, C vehicle is the drug concentration in the vehicle and h is the diffusional path length for the drug. If a cosolvent does not affect membrane barrier prop- erties, its inclusion in the formulation of a weakly acidic drug of the form HX is expected to (1) increase the unionized fraction (HX) of the drug. Since the latter has a higher lipophilicity relative to X – , the overall skin flux, which is proportional to the skin-formulation partition coefficient, K (equation 1) should theoretically be enhanced ; (2) increase drug solubility in the formulation. If the drug concentration is kept constant, this effectively de- creases the degree of saturation and hence should lead to a lower flux. The precise outcome will depend ulti- mately on the nature of the cosolvent, the pH of the formulation and the pK a of the drug. Many non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are or- ganic acids with pK a s ranging from 3 to 5. At the pH of the skin surface (  5), such compounds will be ionized. In the deeper layers of the skin, a less acidic environment is encountered and the degree of ionization of such mol- ecules will therefore increase with consequent effects on solubility and permeation. Ibuprofen is one of the most widely available non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for the management of inflammation and is available in both gel and cream formulations. Although the perme- ation of ibuprofen has been studied from a range of ve- hicles [2] , there has been, to our knowledge, no system- atic investigation into the manner in which formulation components such as cosolvents may influence the ioniza- tion and permeability of ibuprofen into and through the skin. The aim of the present study therefore was to exam- ine the influence of increasing amounts of the cosolvent ethanol on the solubility, ionization and permeation characteristics of ibuprofen through silicone and human skin. As saturated solutions were used throughout this work, a further aim was to investigate the possibility of self-association of ibuprofen in the different cosolvent mixtures. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health- care, Havant, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility of Ibuprofen in Aqueous-Ethanol Mixtures Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were then centrifuged for 15 min at 4,000 g and the supernatant was diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was employed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml  min –1 . The retention time was approximate- ly 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis, and CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Saturated Aqueous Solubility as a Function of pH Excess drug was added to 10 ml of phosphate-citrate buffer [3] at different pH values and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath maintained at 32 ° C. The ionic strength was con- trolled by inclusion of 3 M NaClO 4 in the buffer solutions. Solu- tions were centrifuged for 30 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was diluted and assayed by UV spectroscopy. Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with SD and CV were calculated. pK a and log P and log D Determination The pK a values of ibuprofen were determined using published methods [4, 5] . Ibuprofen was titrated in mixtures of water and ethanol using a GlpKa apparatus (Sirius Analytical Instruments, Forrest Row, UK). The ionic strengths of the solutions were set at 0.15 M with KCl. Yasuda-Shedlovsky plots were used to calculate ‘aqueous’ pK a values [6, 7] . Using octanol as the partition solvent, log P values were also determined by potentiometric titration. The pK a log P program (version 5.1, ACD Ltd.) was also used to predict the distribution coefficients (log D) which were subse- quently compared with the experimentally derived values. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m as de- scribed previously [8] . Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery, obtained following informed consent and with appropri- ate institutional ethical approval was used for skin permeation studies. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [9] . The tissue was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was very carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was immersed in cold water, mounted onto filter paper, and stored in a freezer at –20 ° C until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments (n 6 4) were conducted in Franz dif- fusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, and a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline. The diffusion experiments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. Studies were also con- Downloaded by: Stockholm University Library 130.237.165.40 - 11/14/2018 6:09:37 PM

Watkinson /Herkenne /Guy /Hadgraft / Oliveira /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:15–21 16 where J represents drug flux, D is the drug diffusion co- efficient, K is the partition coefficient for drug between the stratum corneum and the vehicle, C vehicle is the drug concentration in the vehicle and h is the diffusional path length for the drug. If a cosolvent does not affect membrane barrier prop- erties, its inclusion in the formulation of a weakly acidic drug of the form HX is expected to (1) increase the unionized fraction (HX) of the drug. Since the latter has a higher lipophilicity relative to X – , the overall skin flux, which is proportional to the skin-formulation partition coefficient, K (equation 1) should theoretically be enhanced ; (2) increase drug solubility in the formulation. If the drug concentration is kept constant, this effectively de- creases the degree of saturation and hence should lead to a lower flux. The precise outcome will depend ulti- mately on the nature of the cosolvent, the pH of the formulation and the pK a of the drug. Many non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are or- ganic acids with pK a s ranging from 3 to 5. At the pH of the skin surface (  5), such compounds will be ionized. In the deeper layers of the skin, a less acidic environment is encountered and the degree of ionization of such mol- ecules will therefore increase with consequent effects on solubility and permeation. Ibuprofen is one of the most widely available non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for the management of inflammation and is available in both gel and cream formulations. Although the perme- ation of ibuprofen has been studied from a range of ve- hicles [2] , there has been, to our knowledge, no system- atic investigation into the manner in which formulation components such as cosolvents may influence the ioniza- tion and permeability of ibuprofen into and through the skin. The aim of the present study therefore was to exam- ine the influence of increasing amounts of the cosolvent ethanol on the solubility, ionization and permeation characteristics of ibuprofen through silicone and human skin. As saturated solutions were used throughout this work, a further aim was to investigate the possibility of self-association of ibuprofen in the different cosolvent mixtures. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health- care, Havant, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility of Ibuprofen in Aqueous-Ethanol Mixtures Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were then centrifuged for 15 min at 4,000 g and the supernatant was diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was employed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml  min –1 . The retention time was approximate- ly 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis, and CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Saturated Aqueous Solubility as a Function of pH Excess drug was added to 10 ml of phosphate-citrate buffer [3] at different pH values and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath maintained at 32 ° C. The ionic strength was con- trolled by inclusion of 3 M NaClO 4 in the buffer solutions. Solu- tions were centrifuged for 30 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was diluted and assayed by UV spectroscopy. Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with SD and CV were calculated. pK a and log P and log D Determination The pK a values of ibuprofen were determined using published methods [4, 5] . Ibuprofen was titrated in mixtures of water and ethanol using a GlpKa apparatus (Sirius Analytical Instruments, Forrest Row, UK). The ionic strengths of the solutions were set at 0.15 M with KCl. Yasuda-Shedlovsky plots were used to calculate ‘aqueous’ pK a values [6, 7] . Using octanol as the partition solvent, log P values were also determined by potentiometric titration. The pK a log P program (version 5.1, ACD Ltd.) was also used to predict the distribution coefficients (log D) which were subse- quently compared with the experimentally derived values. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m as de- scribed previously [8] . Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery, obtained following informed consent and with appropri- ate institutional ethical approval was used for skin permeation studies. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [9] . The tissue was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was very carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was immersed in cold water, mounted onto filter paper, and stored in a freezer at –20 ° C until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments (n 6 4) were conducted in Franz dif- fusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, and a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline. The diffusion experiments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. Watkinson /Herkenne /Guy /Hadgraft / Oliveira /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:15–21 16 where J represents drug flux, D is the drug diffusion co- efficient, K is the partition coefficient for drug between the stratum corneum and the vehicle, C vehicle is the drug concentration in the vehicle and h is the diffusional path length for the drug. If a cosolvent does not affect membrane barrier prop- erties, its inclusion in the formulation of a weakly acidic drug of the form HX is expected to (1) increase the unionized fraction (HX) of the drug. Since the latter has a higher lipophilicity relative to X – , the overall skin flux, which is proportional to the skin-formulation partition coefficient, K (equation 1) should theoretically be enhanced ; (2) increase drug solubility in the formulation. If the drug concentration is kept constant, this effectively de- creases the degree of saturation and hence should lead to a lower flux. The precise outcome will depend ulti- mately on the nature of the cosolvent, the pH of the formulation and the pK a of the drug. Many non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are or- ganic acids with pK a s ranging from 3 to 5. At the pH of the skin surface (  5), such compounds will be ionized. In the deeper layers of the skin, a less acidic environment is encountered and the degree of ionization of such mol- ecules will therefore increase with consequent effects on solubility and permeation. Ibuprofen is one of the most widely available non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for the management of inflammation and is available in both gel and cream formulations. Although the perme- ation of ibuprofen has been studied from a range of ve- hicles [2] , there has been, to our knowledge, no system- atic investigation into the manner in which formulation components such as cosolvents may influence the ioniza- tion and permeability of ibuprofen into and through the skin. The aim of the present study therefore was to exam- ine the influence of increasing amounts of the cosolvent ethanol on the solubility, ionization and permeation characteristics of ibuprofen through silicone and human skin. As saturated solutions were used throughout this work, a further aim was to investigate the possibility of self-association of ibuprofen in the different cosolvent mixtures. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health- care, Havant, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility of Ibuprofen in Aqueous-Ethanol Mixtures Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were then centrifuged for 15 min at 4,000 g and the supernatant was diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was employed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml  min –1 . The retention time was approximate- ly 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis, and CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Saturated Aqueous Solubility as a Function of pH Excess drug was added to 10 ml of phosphate-citrate buffer [3] at different pH values and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath maintained at 32 ° C. The ionic strength was con- trolled by inclusion of 3 M NaClO 4 in the buffer solutions. Solu- tions were centrifuged for 30 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was diluted and assayed by UV spectroscopy. Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with SD and CV were calculated. pK a and log P and log D Determination The pK a values of ibuprofen were determined using published methods [4, 5] . Ibuprofen was titrated in mixtures of water and ethanol using a GlpKa apparatus (Sirius Analytical Instruments, Forrest Row, UK). The ionic strengths of the solutions were set at 0.15 M with KCl. Yasuda-Shedlovsky plots were used to calculate ‘aqueous’ pK a values [6, 7] . Using octanol as the partition solvent, log P values were also determined by potentiometric titration. The pK a log P program (version 5.1, ACD Ltd.) was also used to predict the distribution coefficients (log D) which were subse- quently compared with the experimentally derived values. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m as de- scribed previously [8] . Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery, obtained following informed consent and with appropri- ate institutional ethical approval was used for skin permeation studies. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [9] . The tissue was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was very carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was immersed in cold water, mounted onto filter paper, and stored in a freezer at –20 ° C until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments (n 6 4) were conducted in Franz dif- fusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, and a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline. The diffusion experiments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. Watkinson /Herkenne /Guy /Hadgraft / Oliveira /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:15–21 16 where J represents drug flux, D is the drug diffusion co- efficient, K is the partition coefficient for drug between the stratum corneum and the vehicle, C vehicle is the drug concentration in the vehicle and h is the diffusional path length for the drug. If a cosolvent does not affect membrane barrier prop- erties, its inclusion in the formulation of a weakly acidic drug of the form HX is expected to (1) increase the unionized fraction (HX) of the drug. Since the latter has a higher lipophilicity relative to X – , the overall skin flux, which is proportional to the skin-formulation partition coefficient, K (equation 1) should theoretically be enhanced ; (2) increase drug solubility in the formulation. If the drug concentration is kept constant, this effectively de- creases the degree of saturation and hence should lead to a lower flux. The precise outcome will depend ulti- mately on the nature of the cosolvent, the pH of the formulation and the pK a of the drug. Many non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are or- ganic acids with pK a s ranging from 3 to 5. At the pH of the skin surface (  5), such compounds will be ionized. In the deeper layers of the skin, a less acidic environment is encountered and the degree of ionization of such mol- ecules will therefore increase with consequent effects on solubility and permeation. Ibuprofen is one of the most widely available non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for the management of inflammation and is available in both gel and cream formulations. Although the perme- ation of ibuprofen has been studied from a range of ve- hicles [2] , there has been, to our knowledge, no system- atic investigation into the manner in which formulation components such as cosolvents may influence the ioniza- tion and permeability of ibuprofen into and through the skin. The aim of the present study therefore was to exam- ine the influence of increasing amounts of the cosolvent ethanol on the solubility, ionization and permeation characteristics of ibuprofen through silicone and human skin. As saturated solutions were used throughout this work, a further aim was to investigate the possibility of self-association of ibuprofen in the different cosolvent mixtures. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health- care, Havant, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility of Ibuprofen in Aqueous-Ethanol Mixtures Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were then centrifuged for 15 min at 4,000 g and the supernatant was diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was employed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml  min –1 . The retention time was approximate- ly 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis, and CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Saturated Aqueous Solubility as a Function of pH Excess drug was added to 10 ml of phosphate-citrate buffer [3] at different pH values and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath maintained at 32 ° C. The ionic strength was con- trolled by inclusion of 3 M NaClO 4 in the buffer solutions. Solu- tions were centrifuged for 30 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was diluted and assayed by UV spectroscopy. Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with SD and CV were calculated. pK a and log P and log D Determination The pK a values of ibuprofen were determined using published methods [4, 5] . Ibuprofen was titrated in mixtures of water and ethanol using a GlpKa apparatus (Sirius Analytical Instruments, Forrest Row, UK). The ionic strengths of the solutions were set at 0.15 M with KCl. Yasuda-Shedlovsky plots were used to calculate ‘aqueous’ pK a values [6, 7] . Using octanol as the partition solvent, log P values were also determined by potentiometric titration. The pK a log P program (version 5.1, ACD Ltd.) was also used to predict the distribution coefficients (log D) which were subse- quently compared with the experimentally derived values. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m as de- scribed previously [8] . Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery, obtained following informed consent and with appropri- ate institutional ethical approval was used for skin permeation studies. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [9] . The tissue was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was very carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was immersed in cold water, mounted onto filter paper, and stored in a freezer at –20 ° C until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments (n 6 4) were conducted in Franz dif- fusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, and a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline. The diffusion experiments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. evidence_items.locator: Section 'Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin'
evidence_items.snippet: Section 'Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin'

Watkinson /Herkenne /Guy /Hadgraft / Oliveira /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:15–21 16 where J represents drug flux, D is the drug diffusion co- efficient, K is the partition coefficient for drug between the stratum corneum and the vehicle, C vehicle is the drug concentration in the vehicle and h is the diffusional path length for the drug. If a cosolvent does not affect membrane barrier prop- erties, its inclusion in the formulation of a weakly acidic drug of the form HX is expected to (1) increase the unionized fraction (HX) of the drug. Since the latter has a higher lipophilicity relative to X – , the overall skin flux, which is proportional to the skin-formulation partition coefficient, K (equation 1) should theoretically be enhanced ; (2) increase drug solubility in the formulation. If the drug concentration is kept constant, this effectively de- creases the degree of saturation and hence should lead to a lower flux. The precise outcome will depend ulti- mately on the nature of the cosolvent, the pH of the formulation and the pK a of the drug. Many non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are or- ganic acids with pK a s ranging from 3 to 5. At the pH of the skin surface (  5), such compounds will be ionized. In the deeper layers of the skin, a less acidic environment is encountered and the degree of ionization of such mol- ecules will therefore increase with consequent effects on solubility and permeation. Ibuprofen is one of the most widely available non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for the management of inflammation and is available in both gel and cream formulations. Although the perme- ation of ibuprofen has been studied from a range of ve- hicles [2] , there has been, to our knowledge, no system- atic investigation into the manner in which formulation components such as cosolvents may influence the ioniza- tion and permeability of ibuprofen into and through the skin. The aim of the present study therefore was to exam- ine the influence of increasing amounts of the cosolvent ethanol on the solubility, ionization and permeation characteristics of ibuprofen through silicone and human skin. As saturated solutions were used throughout this work, a further aim was to investigate the possibility of self-association of ibuprofen in the different cosolvent mixtures. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health- care, Havant, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility of Ibuprofen in Aqueous-Ethanol Mixtures Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were then centrifuged for 15 min at 4,000 g and the supernatant was diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was employed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml  min –1 . The retention time was approximate- ly 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis, and CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Saturated Aqueous Solubility as a Function of pH Excess drug was added to 10 ml of phosphate-citrate buffer [3] at different pH values and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath maintained at 32 ° C. The ionic strength was con- trolled by inclusion of 3 M NaClO 4 in the buffer solutions. Solu- tions were centrifuged for 30 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was diluted and assayed by UV spectroscopy. Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with SD and CV were calculated. pK a and log P and log D Determination The pK a values of ibuprofen were determined using published methods [4, 5] . Ibuprofen was titrated in mixtures of water and ethanol using a GlpKa apparatus (Sirius Analytical Instruments, Forrest Row, UK). The ionic strengths of the solutions were set at 0.15 M with KCl. Yasuda-Shedlovsky plots were used to calculate ‘aqueous’ pK a values [6, 7] . Using octanol as the partition solvent, log P values were also determined by potentiometric titration. The pK a log P program (version 5.1, ACD Ltd.) was also used to predict the distribution coefficients (log D) which were subse- quently compared with the experimentally derived values. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m as de- scribed previously [8] . Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery, obtained following informed consent and with appropri- ate institutional ethical approval was used for skin permeation studies. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [9] . The tissue was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was very carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was immersed in cold water, mounted onto filter paper, and stored in a freezer at –20 ° C until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments (n 6 4) were conducted in Franz dif- fusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, and a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline. The diffusion experiments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. Studies were also con- Downloaded by: Stockholm University Library 130.237.165.40 - 11/14/2018 6:09:37 PM

Results and Discussion Solubility Studies Addition of 25% ethanol increased the solubility of ibuprofen to 343 mg/ml, a 3,800-fold enhancement rela- tive to the drug’s aqueous solubility ( table 1 ). In pure eth- anol, the solubility is 5,500-fold higher than in water. Ibu- profen has a reported pK a of 4.41 [11] , explaining the con- siderable solubility enhanceme

Watkinson /Herkenne /Guy /Hadgraft / Oliveira /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:15–21 16 where J represents drug flux, D is the drug diffusion co- efficient, K is the partition coefficient for drug between the stratum corneum and the vehicle, C vehicle is the drug concentration in the vehicle and h is the diffusional path length for the drug. If a cosolvent does not affect membrane barrier prop- erties, its inclusion in the formulation of a weakly acidic drug of the form HX is expected to (1) increase the unionized fraction (HX) of the drug. Since the latter has a higher lipophilicity relative to X – , the overall skin flux, which is proportional to the skin-formulation partition coefficient, K (equation 1) should theoretically be enhanced ; (2) increase drug solubility in the formulation. If the drug concentration is kept constant, this effectively de- creases the degree of saturation and hence should lead to a lower flux. The precise outcome will depend ulti- mately on the nature of the cosolvent, the pH of the formulation and the pK a of the drug. Many non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are or- ganic acids with pK a s ranging from 3 to 5. At the pH of the skin surface (  5), such compounds will be ionized. In the deeper layers of the skin, a less acidic environment is encountered and the degree of ionization of such mol- ecules will therefore increase with consequent effects on solubility and permeation. Ibuprofen is one of the most widely available non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for the management of inflammation and is available in both gel and cream formulations. Although the perme- ation of ibuprofen has been studied from a range of ve- hicles [2] , there has been, to our knowledge, no system- atic investigation into the manner in which formulation components such as cosolvents may influence the ioniza- tion and permeability of ibuprofen into and through the skin. The aim of the present study therefore was to exam- ine the influence of increasing amounts of the cosolvent ethanol on the solubility, ionization and permeation characteristics of ibuprofen through silicone and human skin. As saturated solutions were used throughout this work, a further aim was to investigate the possibility of self-association of ibuprofen in the different cosolvent mixtures. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health- care, Havant, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility of Ibuprofen in Aqueous-Ethanol Mixtures Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were then centrifuged for 15 min at 4,000 g and the supernatant was diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was employed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml  min –1 . The retention time was approximate- ly 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis, and CV values were ! 5% in all experiments. Saturated Aqueous Solubility as a Function of pH Excess drug was added to 10 ml of phosphate-citrate buffer [3] at different pH values and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath maintained at 32 ° C. The ionic strength was con- trolled by inclusion of 3 M NaClO 4 in the buffer solutions. Solu- tions were centrifuged for 30 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was diluted and assayed by UV spectroscopy. Experiments were performed in triplicate and mean values with SD and CV were calculated. pK a and log P and log D Determination The pK a values of ibuprofen were determined using published methods [4, 5] . Ibuprofen was titrated in mixtures of water and ethanol using a GlpKa apparatus (Sirius Analytical Instruments, Forrest Row, UK). The ionic strengths of the solutions were set at 0.15 M with KCl. Yasuda-Shedlovsky plots were used to calculate ‘aqueous’ pK a values [6, 7] . Using octanol as the partition solvent, log P values were also determined by potentiometric titration. The pK a log P program (version 5.1, ACD Ltd.) was also used to predict the distribution coefficients (log D) which were subse- quently compared with the experimentally derived values. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m as de- scribed previously [8] . Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery, obtained following informed consent and with appropri- ate institutional ethical approval was used for skin permeation studies. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [9] . The tissue was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was very carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was immersed in cold water, mounted onto filter paper, and stored in a freezer at –20 ° C until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments (n 6 4) were conducted in Franz dif- fusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, and a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 . For all permeation experiments the receptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline. The diffusion experiments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. Fluxes were maximum for 70: 30 PG:water systems in silicone membrane; however, for experiments conducted with skin, the flux of ibuprofen systematically increased with increasing amounts of PG. For silicone membrane, the flux values of ibuprofen from ternary ethanol:PG:water (25: 25: 50 and 50: 25: 25) systems

Watkinson /Guy /Hadgraft /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:225–230 226 cinolone acetonide permeability in human skin, Ostren- ga et al. [8] observed that pre-treatment of skin with pure PG caused the skin barrier to become less pliable and at- tributed this effect to dehydration. Zatz and Dalvi [9] in- vestigated benzocaine permeation in hairless mouse skin from a range of solvents and showed that, with water as a standard, interaction effects were not significant when PG was used as a solvent. Hoelgaard and Mollgaard [10] examined the effects of PG on metronidazole flux en- hancement in human skin and suggested a possible ‘car- rier-solvent’ effect. Kasting et al. [11] demonstrated sig- nificant permeation enhancement of triprolidine base from pure PG when compared with oil vehicles in human skin. These workers related this direct effect of PG on the membrane to solvent incorporation in the stratum cor- neum in a sufficient quantity to modify permeant solu- bility in the tissue. As demonstrated previously, topical presentations of agents like ibuprofen which are intended for subcutane- ous action cannot be assumed to be pharmaceutically and clinically equivalent or indeed interchangeable [12] . Moreover, although it is evident that different penetra- tion enhancers influence drug diffusion characteristics to different extents [13] , the complexity of most pharma- ceutical preparations makes it difficult to deconvolve the exact influence of specific enhancers on drug perme- ation. Recently we reported the results of a systematic in- vestigation of the influence of binary systems of ethanol and water on ibuprofen solubility and in vitro permeation characteristics in model membranes and human skin [14] . Analysis of the data confirmed that increasing etha- nol content enhanced the solubility of ibuprofen in both membranes as well as in the vehicle itself. The aim of this work was to extend these studies to explore the influence of PG on ibuprofen permeation and to investigate wheth- er specific effects on partition and diffusion parameters may be distinguished. A secondary goal of the study was to investigate the effects of ethanol in more complex ter- nary systems (PG:ethanol:water) on permeation in sili- cone and skin, as such mixtures are more representative of commercial formulations than simple binary sys- tems. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health Care (Havant, UK). HPLC grade solvents were obtained from Fisher Scientific, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility Determination Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was then diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was performed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml min –1 . The retention time was approximately 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis; coefficients of variation were ! 5% in all experi- ments. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were obtained from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m. Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery was obtained by informed consent and with appropriate institutional ethical approval. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [15] . It was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was then immersed in cold water to aid mounting onto filter paper and stored in a freezer until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments were conducted using saturated solu- tions of ibuprofen for each vehicle using Franz diffusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 and an ap- plication volume of 1 ml. For all permeation experiments the re- ceptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink condi- tions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experi- ments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. The ternary sys- tems were studied under both occluded and non-occluded condi- tions. The number of replicates was at least 4. Fluxes were calculated by monitoring the cumulative amount (Q) of drug diffused as a function of time (t). The permeability coefficient (k p ) was derived from the linear portion of this profile when steady-state transport had been achieved. Partition (  = Kh) and diffusion parameters (  = D/h 2 ) were derived by fitting the entire Q versus t profile to equation 1 [16] . (1) 2 2 2 2 1 1 1 2 exp 6 v n Q c t n t h    d ¯  ¡ °   ¡ ° ¡ ° ¢ ±    where K is the membrane-vehicle partition coefficient of the drug, D is its diffusivity in the membrane of thickness h,  represents Kh, and  represents D/h 2 . Differences between parameters were evaluated using ANOVA with the level of significance set at p ^ 0.05.

Watkinson /Guy /Hadgraft /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:225–230 226 cinolone acetonide permeability in human skin, Ostren- ga et al. [8] observed that pre-treatment of skin with pure PG caused the skin barrier to become less pliable and at- tributed this effect to dehydration. Zatz and Dalvi [9] in- vestigated benzocaine permeation in hairless mouse skin from a range of solvents and showed that, with water as a standard, interaction effects were not significant when PG was used as a solvent. Hoelgaard and Mollgaard [10] examined the effects of PG on metronidazole flux en- hancement in human skin and suggested a possible ‘car- rier-solvent’ effect. Kasting et al. [11] demonstrated sig- nificant permeation enhancement of triprolidine base from pure PG when compared with oil vehicles in human skin. These workers related this direct effect of PG on the membrane to solvent incorporation in the stratum cor- neum in a sufficient quantity to modify permeant solu- bility in the tissue. As demonstrated previously, topical presentations of agents like ibuprofen which are intended for subcutane- ous action cannot be assumed to be pharmaceutically and clinically equivalent or indeed interchangeable [12] . Moreover, although it is evident that different penetra- tion enhancers influence drug diffusion characteristics to different extents [13] , the complexity of most pharma- ceutical preparations makes it difficult to deconvolve the exact influence of specific enhancers on drug perme- ation. Recently we reported the results of a systematic in- vestigation of the influence of binary systems of ethanol and water on ibuprofen solubility and in vitro permeation characteristics in model membranes and human skin [14] . Analysis of the data confirmed that increasing etha- nol content enhanced the solubility of ibuprofen in both membranes as well as in the vehicle itself. The aim of this work was to extend these studies to explore the influence of PG on ibuprofen permeation and to investigate wheth- er specific effects on partition and diffusion parameters may be distinguished. A secondary goal of the study was to investigate the effects of ethanol in more complex ter- nary systems (PG:ethanol:water) on permeation in sili- cone and skin, as such mixtures are more representative of commercial formulations than simple binary sys- tems. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health Care (Havant, UK). HPLC grade solvents were obtained from Fisher Scientific, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility Determination Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was then diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was performed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml min –1 . The retention time was approximately 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis; coefficients of variation were ! 5% in all experi- ments. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were obtained from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m. Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery was obtained by informed consent and with appropriate institutional ethical approval. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [15] . It was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was then immersed in cold water to aid mounting onto filter paper and stored in a freezer until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments were conducted using saturated solu- tions of ibuprofen for each vehicle using Franz diffusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 and an ap- plication volume of 1 ml. For all permeation experiments the re- ceptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink condi- tions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experi- ments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. The ternary sys- tems were studied under both occluded and non-occluded condi- tions. The number of replicates was at least 4. Fluxes were calculated by monitoring the cumulative amount (Q) of drug diffused as a function of time (t). The permeability coefficient (k p ) was derived from the linear portion of this profile when steady-state transport had been achieved. Partition (  = Kh) and diffusion parameters (  = D/h 2 ) were derived by fitting the entire Q versus t profile to equation 1 [16] . (1) 2 2 2 2 1 1 1 2 exp 6 v n Q c t n t h    d ¯  ¡ °   ¡ ° ¡ ° ¢ ±    where K is the membrane-vehicle partition coefficient of the drug, D is its diffusivity in the membrane of thickness h,  represents Kh, and  represents D/h 2 . Differences between parameters were evaluated using ANOVA with the level of significance set at p ^ 0.05.

Watkinson /Guy /Hadgraft /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:225–230 226 cinolone acetonide permeability in human skin, Ostren- ga et al. [8] observed that pre-treatment of skin with pure PG caused the skin barrier to become less pliable and at- tributed this effect to dehydration. Zatz and Dalvi [9] in- vestigated benzocaine permeation in hairless mouse skin from a range of solvents and showed that, with water as a standard, interaction effects were not significant when PG was used as a solvent. Hoelgaard and Mollgaard [10] examined the effects of PG on metronidazole flux en- hancement in human skin and suggested a possible ‘car- rier-solvent’ effect. Kasting et al. [11] demonstrated sig- nificant permeation enhancement of triprolidine base from pure PG when compared with oil vehicles in human skin. These workers related this direct effect of PG on the membrane to solvent incorporation in the stratum cor- neum in a sufficient quantity to modify permeant solu- bility in the tissue. As demonstrated previously, topical presentations of agents like ibuprofen which are intended for subcutane- ous action cannot be assumed to be pharmaceutically and clinically equivalent or indeed interchangeable [12] . Moreover, although it is evident that different penetra- tion enhancers influence drug diffusion characteristics to different extents [13] , the complexity of most pharma- ceutical preparations makes it difficult to deconvolve the exact influence of specific enhancers on drug perme- ation. Recently we reported the results of a systematic in- vestigation of the influence of binary systems of ethanol and water on ibuprofen solubility and in vitro permeation characteristics in model membranes and human skin [14] . Analysis of the data confirmed that increasing etha- nol content enhanced the solubility of ibuprofen in both membranes as well as in the vehicle itself. The aim of this work was to extend these studies to explore the influence of PG on ibuprofen permeation and to investigate wheth- er specific effects on partition and diffusion parameters may be distinguished. A secondary goal of the study was to investigate the effects of ethanol in more complex ter- nary systems (PG:ethanol:water) on permeation in sili- cone and skin, as such mixtures are more representative of commercial formulations than simple binary sys- tems. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health Care (Havant, UK). HPLC grade solvents were obtained from Fisher Scientific, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility Determination Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was then diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was performed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml min –1 . The retention time was approximately 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis; coefficients of variation were ! 5% in all experi- ments. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were obtained from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m. Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery was obtained by informed consent and with appropriate institutional ethical approval. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [15] . It was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was then immersed in cold water to aid mounting onto filter paper and stored in a freezer until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments were conducted using saturated solu- tions of ibuprofen for each vehicle using Franz diffusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 and an ap- plication volume of 1 ml. For all permeation experiments the re- ceptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink condi- tions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experi- ments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. The ternary sys- tems were studied under both occluded and non-occluded condi- tions. The number of replicates was at least 4. Fluxes were calculated by monitoring the cumulative amount (Q) of drug diffused as a function of time (t). The permeability coefficient (k p ) was derived from the linear portion of this profile when steady-state transport had been achieved. Partition (  = Kh) and diffusion parameters (  = D/h 2 ) were derived by fitting the entire Q versus t profile to equation 1 [16] . (1) 2 2 2 2 1 1 1 2 exp 6 v n Q c t n t h    d ¯  ¡ °   ¡ ° ¡ ° ¢ ±    where K is the membrane-vehicle partition coefficient of the drug, D is its diffusivity in the membrane of thickness h,  represents Kh, and  represents D/h 2 . Differences between parameters were evaluated using ANOVA with the level of significance set at p ^ 0.05.

A secondary goal of the study was to investigate the effects of ethanol in more complex ter- nary systems (PG:ethanol:water) on permeation in sili- cone and skin, as such mixtures are more representative of commercial formulations than simple binary sys- tems. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health Care (Havant, UK). HPLC grade solvents were obtained from Fisher Scientific, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility Determination Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was then diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was performed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml min –1 . The retention time was approximately 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis; coefficients of variation were ! 5% in all experi- ments. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were obtained from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m. Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery was obtained by informed consent and with appropriate institutional ethical approval. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [15] . It was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was then immersed in cold water to aid mounting onto filter paper and stored in a freezer until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments were conducted using saturated solu- tions of ibuprofen for each vehicle using Franz diffusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 and an ap- plication volume of 1 ml. For all permeation experiments the re- ceptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink condi- tions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experi- ments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. The ternary sys- tems were studied under both occluded and non-occluded condi- tions. The number of replicates was at least 4. Fluxes were calculated by monitoring the cumulative amount (Q) of drug diffused as a function of time (t). The permeability coefficient (k p ) was derived from the linear portion of this profile when steady-state transport had been achieved. Partition (  = Kh) and diffusion parameters (  = D/h 2 ) were derived by fitting the entire Q versus t profile to equation 1 [16] . (1) 2 2 2 2 1 1 1 2 exp 6 v n Q c t n t h    d ¯  ¡ °   ¡ ° ¡ ° ¢ ±    where K is the membrane-vehicle partition coefficient of the drug, D is its diffusivity in the membrane of thickness h,  represents Kh, and  represents D/h 2 . Differences between parameters were evaluated using ANOVA with the level of significance set at p ^ 0.05.

Watkinson /Guy /Hadgraft /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:225–230 226 cinolone acetonide permeability in human skin, Ostren- ga et al. [8] observed that pre-treatment of skin with pure PG caused the skin barrier to become less pliable and at- tributed this effect to dehydration. Zatz and Dalvi [9] in- vestigated benzocaine permeation in hairless mouse skin from a range of solvents and showed that, with water as a standard, interaction effects were not significant when PG was used as a solvent. Hoelgaard and Mollgaard [10] examined the effects of PG on metronidazole flux en- hancement in human skin and suggested a possible ‘car- rier-solvent’ effect. Kasting et al. [11] demonstrated sig- nificant permeation enhancement of triprolidine base from pure PG when compared with oil vehicles in human skin. These workers related this direct effect of PG on the membrane to solvent incorporation in the stratum cor- neum in a sufficient quantity to modify permeant solu- bility in the tissue. As demonstrated previously, topical presentations of agents like ibuprofen which are intended for subcutane- ous action cannot be assumed to be pharmaceutically and clinically equivalent or indeed interchangeable [12] . Moreover, although it is evident that different penetra- tion enhancers influence drug diffusion characteristics to different extents [13] , the complexity of most pharma- ceutical preparations makes it difficult to deconvolve the exact influence of specific enhancers on drug perme- ation. Recently we reported the results of a systematic in- vestigation of the influence of binary systems of ethanol and water on ibuprofen solubility and in vitro permeation characteristics in model membranes and human skin [14] . Analysis of the data confirmed that increasing etha- nol content enhanced the solubility of ibuprofen in both membranes as well as in the vehicle itself. The aim of this work was to extend these studies to explore the influence of PG on ibuprofen permeation and to investigate wheth- er specific effects on partition and diffusion parameters may be distinguished. A secondary goal of the study was to investigate the effects of ethanol in more complex ter- nary systems (PG:ethanol:water) on permeation in sili- cone and skin, as such mixtures are more representative of commercial formulations than simple binary sys- tems. Materials and Methods Materials Ibuprofen was a generous gift from Wyeth Consumer Health Care (Havant, UK). HPLC grade solvents were obtained from Fisher Scientific, UK. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. Solubility Determination Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was then diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was performed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml min –1 . The retention time was approximately 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis; coefficients of variation were ! 5% in all experi- ments. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were obtained from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m. Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery was obtained by informed consent and with appropriate institutional ethical approval. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [15] . It was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was then immersed in cold water to aid mounting onto filter paper and stored in a freezer until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments were conducted using saturated solu- tions of ibuprofen for each vehicle using Franz diffusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 and an ap- plication volume of 1 ml. For all permeation experiments the re- ceptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink condi- tions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experi- ments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. The ternary sys- tems were studied under both occluded and non-occluded condi- tions. The number of replicates was at least 4. Fluxes were calculated by monitoring the cumulative amount (Q) of drug diffused as a function of time (t). The permeability coefficient (k p ) was derived from the linear portion of this profile when steady-state transport had been achieved. Partition (  = Kh) and diffusion parameters (  = D/h 2 ) were derived by fitting the entire Q versus t profile to equation 1 [16] . Solubility Determination Excess drug was added to each solvent or cosolvent mixture and stirred with a magnetic bar for 48 h in a water bath main- tained at 32 ° C. Solutions were centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was then diluted and assayed either by HPLC or UV spectroscopy. For HPLC analysis, the sample was diluted in isopropanol, followed by appropriate dilution in meth- anol. The stationary phase was an Apex reverse-phase ODS 5-  m packed column (250 ! 4.6 mm) and a mobile phase of 65% ace- tonitrile and 35% phosphate buffer (adjusted to pH 3.2 using or- thophosphoric acid). UV detection was performed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml min –1 . The retention time was approximately 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis; coefficients of variation were ! 5% in all experi- ments. Permeation Studies in Silicone Membrane and Skin The silicone membranes used in this work were obtained from Samco (Nuneaton, UK) with a measured thickness of 400  m. Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery was obtained by informed consent and with appropriate institutional ethical approval. The epidermis was separated by immersing the skin in water maintained at 60 ° C for 1 min [15] . It was then pinned to a corkboard and the epidermis was carefully peeled away from the dermis. The epidermis was then immersed in cold water to aid mounting onto filter paper and stored in a freezer until required. Prior to the diffusion experiment the skin was defrosted and cut to appropriate size. Permeation experiments were conducted using saturated solu- tions of ibuprofen for each vehicle using Franz diffusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 and an ap- plication volume of 1 ml. For all permeation experiments the re- ceptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink condi- tions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experi- ments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. The ternary sys- tems were studied under both occluded and non-occluded condi- tions. The number of replicates was at least 4. Fluxes were calculated by monitoring the cumulative amount (Q) of drug diffused as a function of time (t). The permeability coefficient (k p ) was derived from the linear portion of this profile when steady-state transport had been achieved. Partition (  = Kh) and diffusion parameters (  = D/h 2 ) were derived by fitting the entire Q versus t profile to equation 1 [16] . (1) 2 2 2 2 1 1 1 2 exp 6 v n Q c t n t h    d ¯  ¡ °   ¡ ° ¡ ° ¢ ±    where K is the membrane-vehicle partition coefficient of the drug, D is its diffusivity in the membrane of thickness h,  represents Kh, and  represents D/h 2 . Differences between parameters were evaluated using ANOVA with the level of significance set at p ^ 0.05.

Watkinson /Guy /Hadgraft /Lane Skin Pharmacol Physiol 2009;22:225–230 226 cinolone acetonide permeability in human skin, Ostren- ga et al. [8] observed that pre-treatment of skin with pure PG caused the skin barrier to become less pliable and at- tributed this effect to dehydration. Zatz and Dalvi [9] in- vestigated benzocaine permeation in hairless mouse skin from a range of solvents and showed that, with water as a standard, interaction effects were not significant when PG was used as a solvent. Hoelgaard and Mollgaard [10] examined the effects of PG on metronidazole flux en- hancement in human skin and suggested a possible ‘car- rier-solvent’ effect. Kasting et al. [11] demonstrated sig- nificant permeation enhancement of triprolidine base from pure PG when compared with oil vehicles in human skin. These workers related this direct effect of PG on the membrane to solvent incorporation in the stratum cor- neum in a sufficient quantity to modify permeant solu- bility in the tissue. As demonstrated previously, topical presentations of agents like ibuprofen which are intended for subcutane- ous action cannot be assumed to be pharmaceutically and clinically equivalent or indeed interchangeable [12] . Moreover, although it is evident that different penetra- tion enhancers influence drug diffusion characteristics to different extents [13] , the complexity of most pharma- ceutical preparations makes it difficult to deconvolve the exact influence of specific enhancers on drug perme- ation. Recently we reported the results of a systematic in- vestigation of the influence of binary systems of ethanol and water on ibuprofen solubility and in vitro permeation characteristics in model membranes and human skin [14] . Analysis of the data confirmed that increasing etha- nol content enhanced the solubility of ibuprofen in both membranes as well as in the vehicle itself. The aim of this work was to extend these studies to explore the influence of PG on ibuprofen permeation and to investigate wheth- er specific effects on partition and diffusion parameters may be distinguished. A secondary goal of the study was to investigate the effects of ethanol in more complex ter- nary systems (PG:ethanol:water) on permeation in sili- cone and skin, as such mixtures are more representative of commercial formulations than simple binary sys- tems. All other materials were of reagent grade and were obtained from VWR, UK. UV detection was performed at 225 nm and a flow rate of 1.2 ml min –1 . The retention time was approximately 5 min, and calibration curves were constructed on the basis of peak area measurements using 1 5 standard solutions. Experi- ments were performed in triplicate and mean values with stan- dard deviation and coefficient of variation were calculated. Re- producibility was evaluated prior to injection of the samples and during analysis; coefficients of variation were ! 5% in all experi- ments. Full-thickness human skin from cosmetic surgery was obtained by informed consent and with appropriate institutional ethical approval. The epidermis was then immersed in cold water to aid mounting onto filter paper and stored in a freezer until required. Permeation experiments were conducted using saturated solu- tions of ibuprofen for each vehicle using Franz diffusion cells with a receptor volume of 2.5 ml, a diffusion area of 1 cm 2 and an ap- plication volume of 1 ml. For all permeation experiments the re- ceptor fluid was pH 7.4 phosphate-buffered saline and sink condi- tions were maintained at all time points. The diffusion experi- ments were performed under occluded conditions by sealing the donor compartment with microscope coverslips. The ternary sys- tems were studied under both occluded and non-occluded condi- tions. The number of replicates was at least 4. Fluxes were calculated by monitoring the cumulative amount (Q) of drug diffused as a function of time (t). The permeability coefficient (k p ) was derived from the linear portion of this profile when steady-state transport had been achieved. Partition (  = Kh) and diffusion parameters (  = D/h 2 ) were derived by fitting the entire Q versus t profile to equation 1 [16] . Differences between parameters were evaluated using ANOVA with the level of significance set at p ^ 0.05.
